channel.title: Field Notes on Chinese
channel.description: A field guide to Chinese — characters, language, and the long civilisation behind them.

item.title: 楚辞 · Songs of Chu
item.description: The shamanistic southern voice that gave Chinese literature its second pole — visionary, romantic, and inconsolably exiled.

item.title: 昆曲 · Kun Opera
item.description: The oldest surviving form of Chinese opera — refined to such perfection over four centuries that it nearly disappeared from its own beauty.

item.title: 鲁迅 · Lu Xun
item.description: The writer who looked at traditional Chinese society and saw cannibalism — and the complicated, unresolvable legacy he left behind.

item.title: 诗经 · Book of Songs
item.description: China oldest poetry anthology — three hundred poems that Confucius said no educated person could afford to ignore.

item.title: 水墨画 · Ink-Wash Painting
item.description: Where the brush meets philosophy — five tones of ink, the art of leaving space, and landscapes that are not about landscape at all.

item.title: 宋词 · Song Dynasty Lyric
item.description: The poem that sings — fixed tune patterns, named meters, and three masters who defined what the Chinese lyric could feel.

item.title: 文房四宝 · Four Treasures of the Study
item.description: Brush, ink, inkstone, and paper — the four instruments through which a scholar thought, wrote, painted, and demonstrated who he was.

item.title: 篆刻 · Seal Carving
item.description: The art of the name — bronze to stone, dynasty to dynasty, two schools of carvers, and the small red square that authenticates everything.

item.title: 木 · tree, wood
item.description: A tree seen whole: trunk, branches, roots. The pictograph that became a radical, a wuxing element, and the building block of 林, 森, 本, and 末.

item.title: 水 · water
item.description: The first of the five elements, the radical that runs through hundreds of compounds, and the Daoist image of how to act in the world.

item.title: 王 · king
item.description: Three horizontals joined by one vertical — the ruler who connects heaven, earth, and humanity. Also the most common Chinese surname, and a radical that is secretly 玉 (jade) in disguise.

item.title: 画蛇添足 · Drawing Legs on a Snake
item.description: He finished first, so he kept drawing — and lost the prize. The idiom for ruining a good thing by overdoing it.

item.title: 刻舟求剑 · Carving the Boat to Find the Sword
item.description: He marked the boat where his sword fell into the river. When the boat reached the shore, he dived where the mark was.

item.title: 塞翁失马 · The Old Man at the Frontier Loses His Horse
item.description: His horse ran off. His neighbours grieved. He shrugged. The Daoist parable that became China's word for the unknowability of fortune.

item.title: 守株待兔 · Guarding the Stump, Waiting for Rabbits
item.description: A rabbit ran headlong into a tree stump. The farmer dropped his plough and watched the stump for the rest of his life.

item.title: 愚公移山 · The Foolish Old Man Moves the Mountains
item.description: Two mountains blocked his door. He and his sons began to move them, one basket at a time. A mocker laughed. He said: when I die my sons will continue; when they die, their sons.

item.title: 八大菜系 · Eight Great Cuisines
item.description: How geography, climate, and dynasty shaped eight distinct schools of Chinese cooking.

item.title: 川菜 · Sichuan Cuisine
item.description: The mala kitchen — how a landlocked basin built the most complex spice vocabulary in the world.

item.title: 豆腐 · Tofu
item.description: Two thousand years of soybeans and water: the food that fed a civilization without meat.

item.title: 火锅 · Hotpot
item.description: The communal pot at the center of the table: a social institution disguised as a meal.

item.title: 鲁菜 · Shandong Cuisine
item.description: The oldest school — how the home province of Confucius fed the imperial court for two thousand years.

item.title: 面条 · Noodles
item.description: Four thousand years of wheat in water — noodle cultures from Lanzhou to Suzhou.

item.title: 粤菜 · Cantonese Cuisine
item.description: Freshness as philosophy: the cooking tradition that traveled the world and stayed true to itself.

item.title: 茶文化 · Tea Culture
item.description: Tea as civilization — the way a leaf became a philosophical practice, a diplomatic currency, and the shape of daily life across two millennia.

item.title: 汉服 · Traditional Han Dress
item.description: Ancient silhouettes worn in modern streets — the revival of Han dynasty clothing and the identity politics it carries.

item.title: 红包 · Red Envelopes
item.description: Money wrapped in red — the gift that is never just money, and the ritual that binds generations, occasions, and now hundreds of millions of WeChat users.

item.title: 清明节 · Tomb-Sweeping Day
item.description: The day the living visit the dead — a spring festival of grief, gratitude, and continuity across generations.

item.title: 太极 · Tai Chi
item.description: The slow-motion practice in the park at dawn — its philosophy is older than the movements, and the movements carry the whole philosophy.

item.title: 中秋节 · Mid-Autumn Festival
item.description: The moon at its fullest, the family reunited — the festival that turns a celestial event into a meditation on longing and belonging.

item.title: 称呼 · Forms of Address
item.description: In Chinese, how you address someone is not a formality — it is a precise statement of how you understand the relationship between you.
